## 📋 Program Structure

The school is organized into three weeks, each with a pair of complementary tracks:

| Week | Dates | Track A (Morning) | Track B (Afternoon) |
|------|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Week I** | June 26 – July 1 | Structural Graph Theory | Elementary Graph Algorithms |
| **Week II** | July 3 – July 8 | Graph Decompositions | Parameterized & Approximation Algorithms |
| **Week III** | July 10 – July 15 | Special Graph Classes | Dynamic & Approximation Algorithms |

Each weekday follows a consistent schedule:

| Time | Session |
|------|---------|
| 10:00 – 11:15 | Track A — Lecture |
| 11:15 – 11:45 | ☕ Tea/Coffee |
| 11:45 – 1:00 | Track A — Lecture |
| 1:00 – 2:00 | 🍽️ Lunch |
| 2:00 – 3:15 | Track B — Lecture |
| 3:15 – 3:45 | ☕ Tea/Coffee |
| 3:45 – 5:00 | Track B — Lecture |
| 5:00 – 7:00 | 💻 Tutorials / Labs |

Saturdays are dedicated to hands-on lab sessions.

Co-located with IRISS 2020 and ACM-W Workshop on Women in Computer Science and Research.

## Featured Speakers

### Self-Supervised Learning: The Next Step in AI
**11:00 AM – 12:00 PM** — [Yann LeCun](http://yann.lecun.com/) (NYU, 2018 ACM Turing Award Winner)

Focus on deep learning, self-supervised learning approaches, and latent-variable models for autonomous vehicles.

### Algorithms Beyond the Worst Case
**12:00 PM – 1:00 PM** — Susanne Albers (TU München, Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz Prize)

Refined analysis approaches beyond worst-case performance evaluation.

### New Ways of Thinking of the Mobile Phone for Healthcare
**2:00 PM – 3:00 PM** — Shwetak Patel (University of Washington, 2018 ACM Prize in Computing)

Mobile device sensors for disease screening and management.

### Assistive Technology Solutions for Mobility & Education of Visually Impaired
**3:30 PM – 4:30 PM** — M. Balakrishnan (IIT Delhi, ACM Eugene L. Lawler Award)

ASSISTECH solutions including SmartCane, OnBoard, DotBook.

🎓 About

Graph theory is a classical area of research in combinatorial mathematics that studies mathematical structures used to model pairwise relations between objects. It finds applications across a wide range of domains including computational biology, computational neuroscience, communication network design, VLSI design, traffic optimisation, route planning, computer vision, and network visualisation.

This three-week summer school provides a forum for learning and discussing several foundational aspects of structural graph theory, interleaved with perspectives from algorithmic graph theory. An important emphasis of the curriculum is on the observation that structural results are often exploited to design efficient algorithms. The program features lectures combined with laboratory sessions where participants implement algorithms, learn benchmarking, and work with real-world datasets.

The school was organized by Prof. Anirban Dasgupta and Prof. Neeldhara Misra at IIT Gandhinagar, in partnership with ACM India, with support from the Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO).

📌 Prerequisites

Who should attend?

The school is aimed at advanced undergraduates and beginning graduate students with an interest in graph theory and algorithms.

  • Elementary discrete mathematics and probability
  • Fundamental proof techniques (induction, pigeonhole principle, double counting)
  • Python programming familiarity (preferred for lab sessions)
  • Basic understanding of asymptotic notation (big-O, Theta)
  • A first course in algorithms is beneficial but not mandatory

📚 Lecture Series Overview

Structural Graph Theory (GT)

Graphs and basic definitions; matching; covering and packing; Erdős–Pósa Theorem; connectivity; Menger’s Theorem; Mader’s Theorem; flows and the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem; results from extremal graph theory including Turán’s Theorem.

Elementary Graph Algorithms (GA)

Traversal algorithms (BFS, DFS) and their applications; minimum spanning trees; shortest paths (Dijkstra, Bellman–Ford); maximum matching (Hopcroft–Karp); network flows and applications.

Graph Decompositions (GD)

k-trees; tree decompositions; pathwidth and treewidth; cops and robber games on graphs; brambles; rank-width and other width parameters.

Parameterized & Approximation Algorithms (PA)

Vertex cover — approximation and FPT algorithms; kernelization; feedback vertex set via iterative compression; dynamic programming over tree decompositions; color coding; LP-based kernels.

Special Graph Classes (SGC)

Planar graphs and the Five Color Theorem; Kuratowski’s Theorem; chordal graphs and perfect elimination orderings; geometric graphs; the Perfect Graph Theorem.

Dynamic & Approximation Algorithms (DA)

Introduction to approximation algorithms; 2-approximation for TSP and vertex cover; minimum degree spanning trees; edge coloring; multiway cut (LP relaxation and randomized rounding); hardness of approximation.

## Randomized Methods for Approximation and Parameterized Algorithms **GIAN Course 191014K02 — December 5–9, 2022** A comprehensive 5-day course covering randomized algorithmic techniques for approximation algorithms, parameterized algorithms, and derandomization methods. --- Many important computational problems are NP-complete, making it unlikely that they can be solved optimally in polynomial time. Two major approaches to deal with NP-completeness are approximation algorithms and parameterized algorithms. This course covers how randomization leads to approaches that are faster and easier to implement, through problems such as Longest Path, Minimum Cut, Maximum Cut, Clustering, Vertex Cover, Feedback Vertex Set, and Closest String.
Instructor
**[Daniel Lokshtanov](https://sites.cs.ucsb.edu/~daniello/)**, Professor at UC Santa Barbara. Author of *Kernelization* (2019) and *Parameterized Algorithms* (2015). Expertise in exact exponential algorithms, parameterized algorithms, fine-grained algorithms, and approximation methods. **Co-Instructor:** [Neeldhara Misra](https://www.neeldhara.com/) led select tutorials on probability theory, LPs/SDPs, and tree decompositions.

## Computing Research in Curricula: Knowledge, Education & Training ### How to do Computing Education Research A workshop for CSEd researchers and practitioners (and others) at [IIT Gandhinagar](https://iitgn.ac.in/). **3rd February 2024 (Saturday) — 5th February 2024 (Monday)** --- You likely know what it means to study computing. But what does it mean to study computing *education*? What kinds of questions do we ask, and how do we go about answering them? Join us for the CRiCKET workshop to have a hands-on experience navigating the current and future landscape of CS Education, with a particular emphasis on how *research* (CER: Computing Education Research) in this area works. Though ostensibly about computing education, what we're really going to study is *how to learn things about humans*. Obviously, education is about humans. But several other branches of computing largely or partly do, or could, work with humans too. Obvious candidates are areas like HCI or usable security; less obvious might be areas like formal methods and programming languages; even systems and algorithms have human components. So anyone who is interested in learning about how to learn things about humans will find useful content here. The format will not be slides-and-lectures. There will be virtually no slides, and there will be very little lecturing. Rather, we will do lots of hands-on activities, and we will have several conversations. Most of the learning will take place bottom-up.
Target Audience
The workshop is designed for people who are interested in the methods of learning about humans. You could be a graduate student, postdoc, or faculty member, or a person who works in industry (e.g., EdTech). Either way, you should have a real interest in understanding the methods of working with humans, whether in computing education or some other field.
The Team
The workshop will be led by [Shriram Krishnamurthi](https://cs.brown.edu/~sk/) of Brown University. It is hosted at IIT Gandhinagar, chaired by [Neeldhara Misra](https://www.neeldhara.com) and co-organized by [Venkatesh Choppella](https://faculty.iiit.ac.in/~venkatesh.choppella/).
## Topics Here are the things we want you to learn to think about in computing education research: - What kinds of areas does CER cover? - What kinds of questions does CER ask? - What kinds of study designs do we see? - What kinds of analysis methods are used? - How (and where) are findings presented? ## Schedule

## FAQ
Who is the target audience for this workshop?
The workshop is designed for people who are interested in the methods of learning about humans. You could be a graduate student, postdoc, or faculty member, or work in industry (e.g., EdTech).
Are there any technical pre-requisites?
There are no explicit pre-requisites, although some experience with the instructional aspects of computing classes will be invaluable for those focused on CER.
Can I expect to "get a paper" out of this?
The workshop is intended to train participants in the methods of learning things about humans. You will leave with your head buzzing with ideas for questions to ask, and will have the tools to follow through on them end-to-end. However, this is not to be thought of as a shortcut to a publication.
Will the sessions be open for remote participation and/or recorded?
No. The goal is to have a high-quality intensive, in-person, hands-on event.
What is your DEIA policy?
Diversity and respect for every individual are among the core values of both IIT Gandhinagar and the organizing team. If you face discrimination or harassment of any kind, please report it to the organizers immediately.

Join us for the CRiCKET workshop to have a hands-on experience navigating the current and future landscape of CS Education, with a particular emphasis on how research (CER: Computing Education Research) in this area works.

Though ostensibly about computing education, what we’re really going to study is how to learn things about humans. Obviously, education is about humans. But several other branches of computing largely or partly do, or could, work with humans too. Obvious candidates are areas like HCI or usable security; less obvious might be areas like formal methods and programming languages; even systems and algorithms have human components. So anyone who is interested in learning about how to learn things about humans will find useful content here.

The format will not be slides-and-lectures. There will be virtually no slides, and there will be very little lecturing. Rather, we will do lots of hands-on activities, and we will have several conversations. Most of the learning will take place bottom-up.

Target Audience

The workshop is designed for people who are interested in the methods of learning about humans. You could be a graduate student, postdoc, or faculty member, or a person who works in industry (e.g., EdTech). Either way, you should have a real interest in understanding the methods of working with humans, whether in computing education or some other field.

The Team

The workshop will be led by Shriram Krishnamurthi of Brown University. It is hosted at IIT Gandhinagar, chaired by Neeldhara Misra and co-organized by Venkatesh Choppella.

Topics

Here are the things we want you to learn to think about in computing education research:

  • What kinds of areas does CER cover?
  • What kinds of questions does CER ask?
  • What kinds of study designs do we see?
  • What kinds of analysis methods are used?
  • How (and where) are findings presented?

FAQ

Who is the target audience for this workshop?

The workshop is designed for people who are interested in the methods of learning about humans. You could be a graduate student, postdoc, or faculty member, or work in industry (e.g., EdTech).

Are there any technical pre-requisites?

There are no explicit pre-requisites, although some experience with the instructional aspects of computing classes will be invaluable for those focused on CER.

Can I expect to "get a paper" out of this?

The workshop is intended to train participants in the methods of learning things about humans. You will leave with your head buzzing with ideas for questions to ask, and will have the tools to follow through on them end-to-end. However, this is not to be thought of as a shortcut to a publication.

Will the sessions be open for remote participation and/or recorded?

No. The goal is to have a high-quality intensive, in-person, hands-on event.

What is your DEIA policy?

Diversity and respect for every individual are among the core values of both IIT Gandhinagar and the organizing team. If you face discrimination or harassment of any kind, please report it to the organizers immediately.
